examples.title: First Come First Serve (FCFS) Scheduling
examples.description: Implementation of FCFS CPU scheduling algorithm which selects processes in order of their arrival time
examples.code:
ALGORITHM FCFS_Scheduling
INPUT: List of processes with arrival time and burst time
OUTPUT: Order of execution and average waiting time

BEGIN
  READ n (number of processes)
  FOR i = 1 TO n DO
    READ arrival_time[i], burst_time[i]
    process_id[i] = i
  END FOR

  // Sort by arrival time
  Sort processes by arrival_time in ascending order

  current_time = 0
  total_waiting_time = 0

  FOR i = 1 TO n DO
    IF current_time < arrival_time[i] THEN
      current_time = arrival_time[i]
    END IF
    waiting_time[i] = current_time - arrival_time[i]
    total_waiting_time = total_waiting_time + waiting_time[i]
    current_time = current_time + burst_time[i]
    turnaround_time[i] = current_time - arrival_time[i]
  END FOR

  average_waiting_time = total_waiting_time / n
  DISPLAY "Average Waiting Time:", average_waiting_time
END
examples.explanation: FCFS schedules processes in the order they arrive in the ready queue. It is simple to implement but can cause the convoy effect where short processes wait for long processes.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n log n) due to sorting

examples.title: Round Robin CPU Scheduling
examples.description: Implementation of Round Robin time-sharing scheduling with time quantum
examples.code:
ALGORITHM RoundRobin_Scheduling
INPUT: Processes with arrival time, burst time, time_quantum
OUTPUT: Execution order and average waiting time

BEGIN
  READ n, time_quantum
  FOR i = 1 TO n DO
    READ arrival_time[i], burst_time[i]
    remaining_time[i] = burst_time[i]
    process_id[i] = i
  END FOR

  current_time = 0
  ready_queue = empty
  completed = 0

  WHILE completed < n DO
    // Add all processes that have arrived
    FOR i = 1 TO n DO
      IF arrival_time[i] <= current_time AND i NOT IN ready_queue THEN
        ADD i TO ready_queue
      END IF
    END FOR

    IF ready_queue NOT empty THEN
      current_process = REMOVE_FIRST(ready_queue)

      IF remaining_time[current_process] <= time_quantum THEN
        current_time = current_time + remaining_time[current_process]
        remaining_time[current_process] = 0
        completed = completed + 1
        waiting_time[current_process] = current_time - arrival_time[current_process] - burst_time[current_process]
      ELSE
        current_time = current_time + time_quantum
        remaining_time[current_process] = remaining_time[current_process] - time_quantum
        // Re-add to end of queue
        ADD current_process TO ready_queue
      END IF
    ELSE
      current_time = current_time + 1
    END IF
  END WHILE

  DISPLAY average_waiting_time
END
examples.explanation: Round Robin provides fair time-sharing by allocating a fixed time quantum to each process in circular order. Good for time-sharing systems but overhead of context switching exists.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for process data and queue

examples.title: Process Creation Simulation
examples.description: Demonstrates basic process creation using fork() system call concept in Python
examples.code:
import os
import sys

def child_process():
    """Code executed by child process"""
    print(f"Child Process: PID = {os.getpid()}")
    print("Child is performing its task")
    sys.exit(0)

def parent_process(pid):
    """Code executed by parent process"""
    print(f"Parent Process: PID = {os.getpid()}")
    print(f"Parent waiting for child {pid}")
    os.wait()
    print("Child process terminated")

def main():
    """Demonstrates process creation using os.fork()"""
    print("Original process starting...")
    
    # Fork creates a duplicate process
    pid = os.fork()
    
    if pid == 0:
        # This code runs in child process
        child_process()
    elif pid > 0:
        # This code runs in parent process
        parent_process(pid)
    else:
        # Fork failed
        print("Fork failed!")
        sys.exit(1)
    
    print("Both processes completed")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
examples.explanation: This demonstrates the fork() system call which creates a new process by duplicating the existing process. The child gets a copy of the parent's address space.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is size of parent process memory copied

examples.title: Banker's Algorithm for Deadlock Avoidance
examples.description: Resource allocation and deadlock avoidance algorithm using safe state detection
examples.code:
ALGORITHM Bankers_Algorithm
INPUT: Available resources, Maximum demand, Allocation, Request
OUTPUT: Safe sequence or rejection of request

DATA STRUCTURES:
  Available[1..m]    // Available resources
  Maximum[1..n][1..m] // Maximum demand of each process
  Allocation[1..n][1..m] // Currently allocated resources
  Need[1..n][1..m]    // Remaining resource need

BEGIN
  // Step 1: Calculate Need matrix
  FOR i = 1 TO n DO
    FOR j = 1 TO m DO
      Need[i][j] = Maximum[i][j] - Allocation[i][j]
    END FOR
  END FOR

  // Step 2: Safety Check Algorithm
  FUNCTION IsSafe() RETURNS BOOLEAN
    Work = Available
    Finish[1..n] = FALSE
    safe_sequence = empty

    WHILE TRUE DO
      found = FALSE
      FOR i = 1 TO n DO
        IF Finish[i] = FALSE AND Need[i] <= Work THEN
          // Process can complete
          Work = Work + Allocation[i]
          Finish[i] = TRUE
          ADD i TO safe_sequence
          found = TRUE
        END IF
      END FOR

      IF found = FALSE THEN
        BREAK
      END IF
    END WHILE

    // Check if all processes can finish
    FOR i = 1 TO n DO
      IF Finish[i] = FALSE THEN
        RETURN FALSE
      END IF
    END FOR

    DISPLAY "Safe Sequence:", safe_sequence
    RETURN TRUE
  END FUNCTION

  // Step 3: Resource Request handling
  FUNCTION Request_Resources(process_id, request[])
    IF request <= Need[process_id] THEN
      IF request <= Available THEN
        Available = Available - request
        Allocation[process_id] = Allocation[process_id] + request
        Need[process_id] = Need[process_id] - request

        IF IsSafe() THEN
          RETURN "Request Granted"
        ELSE
          // Rollback
          Available = Available + request
          Allocation[process_id] = Allocation[process_id] - request
          Need[process_id] = Need[process_id] + request
          RETURN "Request Denied - Unsafe State"
        END IF
      ELSE
        RETURN "Process Must Wait - Resources Not Available"
      END IF
    ELSE
      RETURN "Error - Exceeds Maximum Claim"
    END IF
  END FUNCTION
END
examples.explanation: Banker's Algorithm prevents deadlock by ensuring the system remains in a safe state before allocating resources. It requires knowledge of maximum resource needs in advance.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n² × m) for safety check, n=processes, m=resource types
